tweets.text:
BREAKING: Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has surged to 10 million barrels of crude oil per day, per Bloomberg.

Details include:

1. Traffic has increased sharply since the 60-day "Memorandum of Understanding" was signed

2. Oil volumes through the Strait of Hormuz still stand ~50% below pre-war levels

3. Uncertainty still exists around what will happen with the Strait of Hormuz once the MoU expires

US oil prices are down over -40% from the recent high.
tweets.full_text:
BREAKING: Commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has surged to 10 million barrels of crude oil per day, per Bloomberg.

Details include:

1. Traffic has increased sharply since the 60-day "Memorandum of Understanding" was signed

2. Oil volumes through the Strait of Hormuz still stand ~50% below pre-war levels

3. Uncertainty still exists around what will happen with the Strait of Hormuz once the MoU expires

US oil prices are down over -40% from the recent high.

tweets.text:
The long US Dollar trade is crowded:

Speculative long positioning in the US Dollar surged to +$34.3 billion as of June 23rd, the highest in 18 months.

This covers hedge funds and asset managers, who take positions based on price trends and macro views rather than to offset existing currency risk.

Bullish positioning has more than TRIPLED in just 7 weeks.

This also marks the 15th consecutive week of net long positioning, the longest streak since the 21-week run between 2024 and 2025.

Meanwhile, bearish Yen positions held by leveraged funds rose to 115,033 contracts in the week ended June 23rd, near the highest since November 2017.

The long US Dollar trade could reverse soon.
tweets.full_text:

Bullish positioning has more than TRIPLED in just 7 weeks.

This also marks the 15th consecutive week of net long positioning, the longest streak since the 21-week run between 2024 and 2025.

Meanwhile, bearish Yen positions held by leveraged funds rose to 115,033 contracts in the week ended June 23rd, near the highest since November 2017.

The long US Dollar trade could reverse soon.

tweets.text:
Everyone knows that July is seasonally bullish.

But, over the past five years, it is the most negative month for the momentum factor (top 10%/bottom 10%).

Heading into July, momentum has been on a historic tear...makes sense to see the broadening/rotation (excerpt h2 outlook). https://t.co/XIstKBSC2s

tweets.text:
🚢Hormuz Vessel Crossing Update: Recovery Stalls at 19 — Tankers 10, Dry Cargo 9

Commercial vessel crossings through the Strait of Hormuz have slipped to 19 over the latest 24-hour rolling window — 10 tankers and 9 dry cargo ships — down from 24 yesterday and a full 67% below the June 24 recovery peak of 57. What looked like a rebuilding of traffic into late June has flattened into four straight readings stuck in the high-teens-to-20s, well short of anything resembling normal.

Breaking it out by vessel type tells the story of the stall. Both segments spiked on June 24 — 24 tankers and 33 dry cargo ships, each the strongest single-day count since the late-February collapse. But the dry-bulk dash has unwound fastest, off 73% from that peak versus 58% for tankers. The result: tankers have quietly reclaimed the slim majority of the flow (roughly 53%), closer to their pre-conflict share, but on a fraction of the volume.

Powered by our commercial vessel crossing index, using AIS data refreshed every 30 minutes.

Commercial vessel crossings through the Strait of Hormuz have slipped to 19 over the latest 24-hour rolling window — 10 tankers and 9 dry cargo ships — down from 24 yesterday and a full 67% below the June 24 recovery peak of 57. What looked like a rebuilding of traffic into late June has flattened into four straight readings stuck in the high-teens-to-20s, well short of anything resembling normal.

Breaking it out by vessel type tells the story of the stall. Both segments spiked on June 24 — 24 tankers and 33 dry cargo ships, each the strongest single-day count since the late-February collapse. But the dry-bulk dash has unwound fastest, off 73% from that peak versus 58% for tankers. The result: tankers have quietly reclaimed the slim majority of the flow (roughly 53%), closer to their pre-conflict share, but on a fraction of the volume.

tweets.text:
Citi: "Against this backdrop we update our forecasts, in the base case assuming no new [Bitcoin ETF] inflows in the next 12m. This reduces our central BTC forecast to $82k and for ETH to $2.2k. In our prior publications and research we have emphasized the outsized effects of ETF flows in explaining (the variation of) returns - Digital Asset Take: What’s Driving Bitcoin? (It’s Flows). 
The lack of legislative progress and negative sentiment in the sector has seen a reversal of flows YTD. Our targets were predicated on increased investor and financial advisor interest leading to gradual adoption and inflows from these traditional-financial sources. We think this process is likely put on pause until we see a catalyst. The average ETF holder is now underwater and prices are below their pre-2024 US election levels.
Legislation is one potential catalyst but seems unlikely before November’s US midterms. The return of “de-basement fears” could also increase investor interest but Warsh’s hawkish debut reduces those risks in the near-term in our view. Consequently, we reduce our flow assumption in the next 12m to zero in the base case and negative in the bear case. It is important to note that crypto markets are volatile, and sentiment driven which makes them reflexive in terms of price action, on-chain/off-chain activity, and flows. This increases forecast uncertainty but can cause abrupt changes in sentiment."
tweets.full_text:
Citi: "Against this backdrop we update our forecasts, in the base case assuming no new [Bitcoin ETF] inflows in the next 12m. This reduces our central BTC forecast to $82k and for ETH to $2.2k. In our prior publications and research we have emphasized the outsized effects of ETF flows in explaining (the variation of) returns - Digital Asset Take: What’s Driving Bitcoin? (It’s Flows). 
The lack of legislative progress and negative sentiment in the sector has seen a reversal of flows YTD. Our targets were predicated on increased investor and financial advisor interest leading to gradual adoption and inflows from these traditional-financial sources. We think this process is likely put on pause until we see a catalyst. The average ETF holder is now underwater and prices are below their pre-2024 US election levels.
Legislation is one potential catalyst but seems unlikely before November’s US midterms. The return of “de-basement fears” could also increase investor interest but Warsh’s hawkish debut reduces those risks in the near-term in our view. Consequently, we reduce our flow assumption in the next 12m to zero in the base case and negative in the bear case. It is important to note that crypto markets are volatile, and sentiment driven which makes them reflexive in terms of price action, on-chain/off-chain activity, and flows. This increases forecast uncertainty but can cause abrupt changes in sentiment."
